instruction:
Here are the TDR measurements from today's production run on a 90 mm differential pair routed on layers 3-4 of our 8-layer stack-up. Target impedance is 50 ohm. Please review.

TDR impedance sweep (target Z0 = 50 ohm):
  Distance (mm)    Z (ohm)
             0      50.3
             4      49.8
             8      50.1
            12      49.6
            16      50.4
            20      50.2
            24      49.7
            28      50.5
            32      50.1
            36      49.9
            40      50.3
            44      41.2
            48      38.5
            52      42.8
            56      50.1
            60      49.7
            64      50.4
            68      50.0
            72      49.8
            76      50.2
            80      50.5
            84      49.9
            88      50.1
            90      50.3
output:
This TDR sweep reveals a significant localized impedance anomaly that requires attention.

Detection: The impedance profile is well-controlled at 50 ohm +/- 1 ohm across most of the trace, but there is a sharp dip between 44 mm and 52 mm, reaching a minimum of 38.5 ohm at the 48 mm mark. This represents a 23% deviation from the 50 ohm target, far exceeding the typical +/- 10% tolerance for controlled-impedance traces. The anomaly spans approximately 8 mm and has a symmetric shape — dipping to 41.2 ohm, bottoming at 38.5 ohm, then recovering through 42.8 ohm back to nominal.

Isolation: The physics of this anomaly points to a localized capacitance increase. Characteristic impedance Z0 = sqrt(L/C), so a reduction in Z0 from 50 to 38.5 ohm implies a roughly 69% increase in per-unit-length capacitance at that location (since (50/38.5)^2 = 1.69). The spatial extent of 8 mm and the location at 44-52 mm are consistent with a layer transition via field. When a differential pair transitions between layers through vias, the via pads and antipads add parallel-plate capacitance that reduces the local impedance. The via pad diameter, antipad clearance, and stub length all contribute. A back-drill stub residual of 15-20 mils in FR4 would produce exactly this magnitude of impedance dip.

Alternatively, if there is no via transition at this location per the layout, the anomaly could indicate a dielectric thickness reduction (thinner prepreg) or an unintended copper pour encroachment near the trace at that point, both of which increase capacitance.

Recommended action: Cross-reference the 44-52 mm location against the PCB layout to confirm whether a via transition exists there. If it does, evaluate the back-drill depth — the residual stub should be less than 10 mils to minimize capacitive loading. If no via exists at that location, request a cross-section at the 48 mm point to measure the actual dielectric thickness and trace-to-plane spacing. For immediate production disposition, this impedance dip will cause signal reflections with a reflection coefficient of rho = (38.5 - 50)/(38.5 + 50) = -0.13, which may be acceptable for data rates below 5 Gbps but will degrade eye height at 10 Gbps and above.

instruction:
We just completed the boundary scan interconnect test suite on a new board revision. Here are the results from 10 consecutive runs across 200 nets. Can you take a look at the data?

Boundary Scan Interconnect Test — 10 runs, 200 nets:
  Run  1: 200/200 PASS
  Run  2: 197/200 PASS (FAIL: NET_147, NET_148, NET_149)
  Run  3: 200/200 PASS
  Run  4: 198/200 PASS (FAIL: NET_147, NET_148)
  Run  5: 200/200 PASS
  Run  6: 197/200 PASS (FAIL: NET_147, NET_148, NET_149)
  Run  7: 200/200 PASS
  Run  8: 199/200 PASS (FAIL: NET_148)
  Run  9: 200/200 PASS
  Run 10: 198/200 PASS (FAIL: NET_147, NET_149)

Net mapping:
  NET_147: U3.B7 (FPGA) → U8.pin14 (DDR addr A12)
  NET_148: U3.B8 (FPGA) → U8.pin15 (DDR addr A13)
  NET_149: U3.B9 (FPGA) → U8.pin16 (DDR addr A14)
output:
This data shows a pattern that is diagnostic of a specific physical mechanism.

Detection: Three adjacent nets — NET_147, NET_148, and NET_149 — fail intermittently across the 10 runs, while all other 197 nets pass consistently. The failure rate is approximately 40% per run for these three nets, and they always fail together or in subsets (never does NET_149 fail without NET_147 or NET_148 also being suspect). The intermittent nature — passing on some runs, failing on others with no changes to the DUT — rules out a hard defect like an open or short, which would fail 100% of the time.

Isolation: These three nets are physically adjacent address lines (A12, A13, A14) connecting the FPGA ball array positions B7-B9 to DDR address pins 14-16. The ball pitch at B7-B9 on a typical FPGA BGA is 0.5-0.8 mm, meaning the breakout traces from these balls run in close parallel with very tight spacing — typically 4-5 mils trace-to-trace. The physics at play is capacitive crosstalk: mutual capacitance C_m between adjacent parallel traces couples voltage from the aggressor (the net being driven by boundary scan EXTEST) to the victim (adjacent quiet net). The coupled voltage V_victim = C_m / (C_self + C_m) * V_aggressor. When this coupled voltage exceeds the receiver input threshold V_IH(min), the boundary scan captures an incorrect logic state, which the test interprets as a short.

The intermittency arises because boundary scan drive strength, power supply noise, and temperature all modulate the coupling margin by a few tens of millivolts — sometimes the coupled signal stays below the threshold, sometimes it exceeds it.

Recommended action: First, confirm the hypothesis by re-running the interconnect test at reduced TCK frequency (1 MHz instead of the default 5-10 MHz) and with modified test vectors that drive adjacent nets to known states rather than leaving them floating. If failures disappear, crosstalk is confirmed. Second, review the FPGA breakout routing on the PCB — increase spacing between A12/A13/A14 traces to at least 3x the dielectric height (the 3W rule) if layout allows. If not, add ground-referenced guard traces between the address lines. For production, this can be dispositioned as a test limitation rather than a defect, documented with an engineering waiver noting that the crosstalk level does not affect functional operation at DDR4 data rates because the DDR controller uses different timing than boundary scan.

instruction:
Routine thermal survey on the new power supply module. Six thermocouples placed across the board in the forced-air cooling zone. System has been at steady-state for 20 minutes under nominal load. Here are the readings.

  TC1 (inlet side):   52.3 C
  TC2 (mid-left):     53.1 C
  TC3 (mid-right):    52.8 C
  TC4 (outlet side):  64.7 C
  TC5 (center-rear):  65.2 C
  TC6 (far outlet):   66.1 C

Ambient: 24.5 C
Airflow: 2.0 m/s nominal at inlet
output:
This thermal survey reveals a significant temperature gradient that should not exist in a uniform forced-air cooling zone.

Detection: Thermocouples TC1-TC3 (inlet side and mid-board) read 52.3-53.1 C, forming a tight cluster with less than 1 C spread — this is the expected behavior for uniform convective cooling. However, TC4-TC6 (outlet side and center-rear) read 64.7-66.1 C, a full 12-13 C higher. The design intent for this zone is uniform airflow producing readings within 3 C of each other. A 13 C gradient across a zone designed for uniform cooling is a clear anomaly. Under normal forced-air conditions with 2.0 m/s airflow, the air temperature rise from inlet to outlet should be delta_T_air = P / (rho * Cp * V * A), which for a typical module dissipating 15-20W would produce only 3-5 C rise.

Isolation: The sharp spatial transition between TC3 (52.8 C) and TC4 (64.7 C) indicates an airflow obstruction rather than a gradual thermal effect. If the issue were elevated power dissipation from a single component, we would see a localized hot spot centered on that component with radial gradients. Instead, we see a planar boundary — everything downstream of mid-board is uniformly hot. This pattern is characteristic of blocked or diverted airflow. The convective heat transfer coefficient h is directly proportional to airflow velocity; if airflow is blocked in the TC4-TC6 region, the local h drops dramatically, and the temperature rises until conduction paths to the cooled region compensate. The most likely physical cause is a misaligned heatsink clip, shifted baffle, or mechanical interference that creates an airflow shadow zone covering the outlet half of the board.

Recommended action: Visually inspect the cooling path for mechanical obstructions — check heatsink clip positions, cable routing, and any baffles or air guides. Measure actual airflow velocity at the TC4-TC6 locations using an anemometer; expect to see significantly reduced velocity compared to the 2.0 m/s at the inlet. If an obstruction is found, correct it and re-measure; the TC4-TC6 readings should drop to within 3 C of TC1-TC3. If no visible obstruction exists, the system-level airflow design may need CFD analysis to verify that the duct geometry provides uniform flow across the full board area.

instruction:
Running our weekly calibration check. Here's the S21 insertion loss measurement on channel 3 of the backplane, 6-inch differential pair. Measured from 100 MHz to 20 GHz using the VNA.

S21 Insertion Loss (Channel 3, 6-inch diff pair):
  Freq (GHz)    S21 (dB)
        0.1      -0.4
        0.5      -1.1
        1.0      -1.8
        2.0      -3.2
        3.0      -4.5
        4.0      -6.1
        5.0      -8.3
        6.0     -10.2
        7.0     -12.8
        8.0     -15.1
        8.5     -22.7
        9.0     -15.8
       10.0     -13.9
       12.0     -16.5
       14.0     -19.2
       16.0     -22.1
       18.0     -25.4
       20.0     -28.8

Channel model predicts -8 dB at 10 GHz for this trace length.
output:
This S21 measurement has two distinct anomalies that both point to the same root cause.

Detection: First, the overall insertion loss is significantly higher than the channel model predicts. The model expects -8 dB at 10 GHz, but the measurement shows -13.9 dB — nearly 6 dB of excess loss. This excess grows with frequency: at 5 GHz the expected loss would be approximately -4 dB based on the sqrt(f) skin-effect scaling, but the measurement shows -8.3 dB. Second, there is a sharp resonant notch at 8.5 GHz where the insertion loss plunges to -22.7 dB — a sudden 7-8 dB drop below the already-elevated loss trend, recovering immediately at 9.0 GHz. This narrowband notch is not part of normal dielectric or conductor loss, which produces monotonically increasing attenuation.

Isolation: The resonant notch at 8.5 GHz is the signature of a quarter-wave stub resonance. A via stub acts as a short-circuited transmission line that resonates at f = c / (4 * L_stub * sqrt(epsilon_r)). Working backward: for FR4 with epsilon_r = 3.8, the propagation velocity is approximately 1.54 * 10^8 m/s. A resonance at 8.5 GHz implies L_stub = v_p / (4 * f) = 1.54e8 / (4 * 8.5e9) = 4.5 mm, which is approximately 177 mils. This is consistent with a backplane via that was designed for back-drilling but has a residual stub of approximately 30 mils (the difference between the full via length through a thick backplane and the back-drill depth). The excess broadband loss above 4 GHz is also caused by the stub — even away from the resonant frequency, the stub creates a reactive loading that increases energy coupling into the stub and raises overall attenuation.

Recommended action: Verify the back-drill depth on channel 3 vias. The stub residual should be less than 10 mils for 10+ Gbps operation — the current 30 mil residual is unacceptable. Request a cross-section of the via to measure actual drill depth. If this is a systematic back-drill issue across the panel, all channels need re-measurement. For immediate use, this channel will not support 25 Gbps NRZ signaling but may be marginally usable at 10 Gbps if the equalizer can compensate for the 6 dB excess loss. The notch at 8.5 GHz will alias energy at that frequency into adjacent symbols regardless of equalization.

instruction:
Here's the flying probe test report from this morning's production lot. 50 test points measured with 4-wire technique. I've included the XY coordinates for each point. Standard review requested.

Flying Probe 4-Wire Resistance — Lot 2026-W11-003:
  Point  Component   Nominal    Measured    XY (mm)       Status
  TP01   R1          10.0k      10.04k      (25, 30)      PASS
  TP02   R5          4.70k       4.73k      (50, 45)      PASS
  TP03   R12         1.00k       1.01k      (75, 60)      PASS
  TP04   R18        47.0k      47.2k        (120, 80)     PASS
  TP05   R22        10.0k      10.03k       (150, 100)    PASS
  TP06   R27         2.20k       2.22k      (30, 120)     PASS
  TP07   R31       100.0k     101.2k        (55, 145)     PASS
  TP08   R35         4.70k       4.74k      (80, 155)     PASS
  TP09   R38        10.0k      10.8k        (40, 165)     FAIL
  TP10   R41        22.0k      23.5k        (25, 175)     FAIL
  TP11   R44         1.00k       1.07k      (60, 180)     FAIL
  TP12   R47        47.0k      50.9k        (35, 190)     FAIL
  TP13   R50        10.0k      10.05k       (140, 170)    PASS
  TP14   R53         4.70k       4.71k      (160, 180)    PASS
  ... (remaining 36 points in lower-right quadrant: all PASS, within 1% of nominal)
output:
This flying probe report contains a spatially correlated failure pattern that reveals a fixture issue rather than component defects.

Detection: Four test points fail — TP09 through TP12 — all reading 7-8% above their nominal values. The key observation is that these are different component values (10k, 22k, 1k, 47k) from different component families, yet all read high by a similar percentage. Meanwhile, components in other board regions pass within 1% of nominal, including the same resistance values (TP01 and TP05 are also 10.0k but read correctly at 10.04k and 10.03k). This rules out component tolerance issues — if R38 (10k) were genuinely 10.8k, that would be a 8% error on a 1% or 5% resistor, but identical-value resistors elsewhere are fine.

Isolation: Plotting the XY coordinates reveals the pattern. All four failing points cluster in the upper-left region: X ranges from 25-60 mm, Y ranges from 165-190 mm. All passing points are either at lower Y values or at higher X values. This spatial correlation is the hallmark of a probe contact force problem. The contact resistance at each probe tip is R_contact = rho / (pi * a), where a is the effective contact spot radius. Reduced probe force decreases a, adding series resistance to every measurement in the affected zone. A 4-wire measurement should reject contact resistance, but if the voltage-sense probes also have reduced contact force, the sense point shifts away from the component, and trace resistance between the component and the sense point adds to the reading. The consistent 7-8% positive bias across different component values confirms a systematic additive error proportional to the measurement, consistent with a gain error from probe positioning in that zone.

The root cause is vacuum plate warpage or fixture planarity loss in the upper-left quadrant, causing probes in that region to have reduced contact force and increased contact resistance.

Recommended action: Perform a fixture planarity check using a surface gauge or indicator across the vacuum plate. Inspect the upper-left quadrant probes for wear, bent tips, or contamination. Re-run the failing points with manual probe force verification. If readings normalize with corrected probe force, schedule fixture maintenance — replace worn probes and re-level the vacuum plate. Do not reject this lot based on these results; the failures are measurement artifacts, not component defects.

instruction:
Attached are the POST diagnostic results from our latest board bring-up. The self-test ran through all subsystems. Could you review the output?

Power-On Self-Test (POST) Results — Board SN 2026-0847:
  Step 01 — CPU core:          PASS (code 0x00)
  Step 02 — L1/L2 cache:       PASS (code 0x00)
  Step 03 — PCIe link training: PASS (code 0x00)
  Step 04 — Memory init:        PASS (code 0x00)
  Step 05 — Memory pattern test: ERROR (code 0x42)
  Step 06 — GPIO loopback:      PASS (code 0x00)
  Step 07 — Ethernet PHY:       PASS (code 0x00)
  Step 08 — USB enumeration:    PASS (code 0x00)

ECC Error Register (captured during Step 05):
  Bank 0: 0 correctable errors / 0 uncorrectable
  Bank 1: 0 correctable errors / 0 uncorrectable
  Bank 2: 14 correctable errors / 0 uncorrectable
  Bank 3: 0 correctable errors / 0 uncorrectable

Error addresses (Bank 2): Row 0x1A3F repeated in 12 of 14 errors
Test coverage: 256 MB per bank, full march-C pattern
output:
This POST report has a clear anomaly that isolates to a specific physical location in the memory subsystem.

Detection: All POST steps pass except Step 05 (memory pattern test), which returns error code 0x42. The ECC register shows 14 single-bit correctable errors, all concentrated in Bank 2 — the other three banks have zero errors across a full 256 MB march-C test. Furthermore, 12 of the 14 errors map to the same row address 0x1A3F, indicating the errors are not randomly distributed across Bank 2 but are localized to a specific row. The fact that these are all single-bit correctable (no multi-bit uncorrectable) errors is significant — it tells us this is an isolated cell-level weakness, not a row decoder failure or column line defect, which would produce multi-bit patterns.

Isolation: DRAM stores each bit as charge on a small capacitor: Q = C_cell * V. During the refresh interval (typically 64 ms for DDR4 at normal temperatures), the cell must retain enough charge for the sense amplifier to read the correct state. A weak cell has either reduced storage capacitance (manufacturing defect in the trench or stack capacitor) or elevated junction leakage current at the access transistor. Either condition shortens the retention time below the refresh interval, causing the cell to lose its data before the next refresh cycle. The concentration of errors at row 0x1A3F indicates a specific physical row in Bank 2 where one or more cells have marginal retention. The march-C pattern stresses this by writing and reading back patterns that require cells to hold state through multiple read-modify-write cycles.

The fact that 14 errors occur (not hundreds) and they are all single-bit correctable confirms this is an isolated weak-cell phenomenon — likely 1-2 physical cells in the same row that flip depending on the data pattern and timing of the march sequence.

Recommended action: Run an extended retention time test on Bank 2 by reducing the refresh rate to 2x the normal interval (128 ms) and re-running the pattern test — this will amplify weak cells and reveal if there are borderline cells beyond the 14 already detected. If the board must ship, the weak row can be mapped out via the memory controller's address remapping feature if available. For root cause, this is likely a DRAM die defect rather than a solder or signal integrity issue, since the errors are row-specific and single-bit. Check if other boards from the same DRAM lot show similar behavior — if so, escalate to the DRAM supplier with the specific row address and bank information.

instruction:
Can you look over this code density histogram from the ADC characterization we ran this morning? Standard ramp test, 12-bit SAR ADC, 1 million samples.

12-bit SAR ADC Code Density Test (1,048,576 samples, linear ramp input):
  Expected count per code: ~256 (uniform distribution)

  Code range     Avg count    Min count    Max count
  0000-0500        257          241          272
  0501-1000        255          238          270
  1001-1500        258          240          275
  1501-2000        254          237          271
  2001-2046        256          242          269
  2047              0            0            0
  2048              0            0            0
  2049              0            0            0
  2050            518          518          518
  2051-2500       257          239          273
  2501-3000       255          241          268
  3001-3500       258          243          272
  3501-4095       256          240          269

  Total missing codes: 3 (codes 2047, 2048, 2049)
  Code 2046: count = 512
  Code 2050: count = 518
output:
This code density histogram reveals a critical ADC defect centered at the mid-scale transition.

Detection: Three consecutive codes — 2047, 2048, and 2049 — have zero occurrences in over one million samples with a linear ramp input. In a properly functioning 12-bit ADC, each of the 4096 codes should appear approximately 256 times with a uniform ramp. Instead, these three codes never appear, and the adjacent codes 2046 and 2050 have counts of 512 and 518 respectively — almost exactly double the expected count. This means the input voltage ranges that should produce codes 2047-2049 are being mapped to either 2046 or 2050, effectively creating a "dead zone" in the transfer function spanning 3 LSBs at mid-scale.

Isolation: The location of this defect is not random — it occurs precisely at the code 2048 boundary, which is the MSB (bit 11) transition point in a 12-bit SAR ADC. During the successive approximation process, the MSB is the first bit resolved. The internal DAC sets its MSB capacitor (C_MSB = 2048 * C_unit) and the comparator decides whether the input is above or below the mid-scale reference voltage. If the comparator has an offset voltage that exceeds the quantization step size (V_LSB = V_ref / 4096), it will make incorrect decisions at the MSB boundary. Specifically, a comparator offset of approximately 3 * V_LSB (about 2.4 mV for a 3.3V reference) would cause codes 2047-2049 to be skipped entirely, with all inputs in that range being rounded to 2046 or 2050.

An alternative root cause is a capacitor mismatch in the SAR DAC array. If C_MSB deviates from its ideal value by more than 0.1%, the DAC output at code 2048 shifts by more than 1 LSB, creating a gap in the transfer function. The capacitor mismatch hypothesis is supported by the fact that exactly 3 codes are missing — this corresponds to the MSB capacitor being approximately 0.15% from nominal.

Recommended action: This ADC fails its missing-code specification and should not be used for precision measurement. If this is an evaluation unit, characterize the comparator offset by measuring the actual MSB transition voltage using a precision ramp and high-resolution voltmeter. If multiple ADCs from the same lot show the same defect at the same code boundary, it indicates a systematic comparator offset from the fabrication process — escalate to the silicon vendor. If it is unit-specific, the part is a manufacturing outlier. For system-level mitigation, if the ADC must be used, software calibration can interpolate across the missing codes, but this degrades effective resolution to approximately 11 bits around mid-scale.

instruction:
Here is the Icc quiescent current data for U7 from our latest production lot. We collect this on every board at ICT. Wanted your routine analysis.

Icc (quiescent supply current) — U7, 3.3V LDO regulator:
  Historical baseline (first 2,000 boards): mean = 42.3 mA, sigma = 2.8 mA
  Current lot (last 350 boards): mean = 65.1 mA, sigma = 2.9 mA
  Shift: +22.8 mA (54% above historical mean)
  Sigma unchanged — distribution shifted, not broadened
  All readings within current test limits (10 mA to 150 mA)
output:
This Icc distribution data contains a significant systematic shift that warrants investigation despite passing current test limits.

Detection: The quiescent supply current for U7 has shifted from a historical mean of 42.3 mA to 65.1 mA — an increase of 22.8 mA, representing a 54% rise. Critically, the standard deviation is unchanged (2.8 mA vs 2.9 mA), which tells us the distribution has translated upward without broadening. This is a rigid shift of the entire population, not a tail effect or increased scatter. While all readings technically pass the test limits of 10-150 mA, this shift is physically meaningful and should not be ignored simply because it falls within wide test limits.

Isolation: The stable sigma is the key diagnostic clue. If the increased current were caused by random defects — such as solder shorts, contamination, or damaged devices — we would expect sigma to increase because defects affect units randomly and by varying amounts. Instead, the constant sigma with shifted mean indicates a deterministic, systematic cause that affects every unit equally. The physics narrows this to two possibilities. First, a component lot change: if the 3.3V LDO regulator silicon was sourced from a different wafer lot or fabrication revision, the internal bias currents and quiescent operating point may have shifted. The 22.8 mA increase represents additional power dissipation of P = 22.8 mA * 3.3V = 75 mW, which is significant for thermal budget but may be within the device specification. Second, a design revision change: if the board revision changed passive component values in the LDO feedback network, the output voltage or operating point could shift, changing quiescent current.

The 54% increase is large enough to affect thermal performance. The additional 75 mW raises the junction temperature by delta_T = P * theta_JA, which for a typical SOT-223 package (theta_JA approximately 65 C/W) means approximately 5 C hotter. This reduces thermal margin and could trigger failures under worst-case ambient conditions.

Recommended action: Pull the component date codes from the current lot and compare against the baseline lot. If the LDO supplier or lot changed, request the supplier's characterization data for the new lot to verify the Icc shift is within their device specification. Tighten the test limits to flag this shift — the current 10-150 mA window is too wide to catch a 54% shift. A limit of mean +/- 4 sigma from the historical baseline (approximately 31-54 mA) would have flagged this immediately. Update the thermal analysis to account for the additional 75 mW dissipation.

instruction:
Pulled these thermocouple readings from the MOSFET power stage during our standard 120-second burn-in test. Q3 is the main switching FET. Can you review?

Thermocouple on Q3 (power MOSFET), powered test profile:
  Time (s)    Temp (C)
         0      24.2
         5      36.8
        10      45.3
        15      52.1
        20      57.4
        25      61.5
        30      64.8
        35      68.2
        40      72.1
        45      76.5
        50      81.7
        55      87.6
        60      94.3
        65     102.1
        70     111.4
        75     122.3
        80     134.9
        85     149.2
        90     165.1

Test was manually aborted at 90s due to over-temperature alarm (limit: 150 C).
Ambient: 24.2 C, load: nominal 12V/5A
output:
This temperature profile shows a dangerous thermal runaway condition that required the emergency shutoff.

Detection: A properly functioning power MOSFET under constant load should reach thermal equilibrium — the temperature rises exponentially with a thermal time constant tau (typically 10-20 seconds for a well-heatsinked TO-220 or D2PAK) and asymptotically settles to a steady-state value T_ss = T_ambient + P * theta_JA. Looking at this data, the initial response from 0-30 seconds follows a normal exponential approach: rising from 24.2 C to 64.8 C, which would suggest a steady-state around 70-75 C. However, after 30 seconds the temperature does not settle — it continues accelerating. From 30-60 seconds it rises another 29.5 C (64.8 to 94.3), and from 60-90 seconds it rises 70.8 C (94.3 to 165.1). The rate of temperature rise is itself increasing: approximately 1.0 C/s at t=30, 1.4 C/s at t=60, and 2.4 C/s at t=80. An exponentially increasing rate of temperature rise is the defining signature of thermal runaway.

Isolation: Thermal runaway in a power MOSFET occurs through positive feedback between temperature and power dissipation. The primary mechanism is parasitic BJT activation. Every power MOSFET contains an inherent parasitic NPN bipolar transistor formed by the source (emitter), body (base), and drain (collector) regions. As temperature increases, the substrate leakage current rises exponentially per the Arrhenius relationship: I_leak is proportional to exp(-Ea/kT). This leakage acts as base current for the parasitic NPN. At some critical temperature, the NPN gain (beta) multiplied by the leakage current produces enough collector current to sustain itself — the device latches on independently of the gate drive. Once this positive feedback loop engages, power dissipation increases with temperature, which increases leakage, which increases power — the classic thermal runaway spiral.

The data shows the transition from normal thermal behavior to runaway occurring around t=30-40 seconds, when the junction temperature likely exceeded 100-110 C (accounting for the case-to-junction thermal resistance, the junction is 15-25 C hotter than the thermocouple reading).

Recommended action: Do not re-test this unit — the MOSFET may be permanently degraded from the thermal event. Verify the heatsink thermal interface material (TIM) is properly applied; a dry or voided TIM increases theta_JA and triggers runaway at a lower power level. Check that the gate drive voltage is within specification — insufficient V_GS increases R_DS(on), raising conduction losses. If TIM and gate drive are correct, the MOSFET may be undersized for the 12V/5A load (60W); calculate the actual power dissipation P = I^2 * R_DS(on) and verify it is within the device SOA with adequate derating.

instruction:
Monthly PDN impedance check on the main processor board. VNA measurement at the U1 VDD pad. Standard sweep from 1 MHz to 1 GHz. Please review.

PDN impedance Z(f) at U1 VDD pad:
  Freq (MHz)      |Z| (ohm)
           1      0.042
           5      0.038
          10      0.055
          20      0.081
          50      0.145
         100      0.310
         120      0.680
         140      1.850
         150      2.240
         160      1.920
         180      0.750
         200      0.380
         250      0.285
         300      0.320
         400      0.410
         500      0.490
         600      0.385
         800      0.340
        1000      0.380

  Target impedance: < 0.5 ohm from DC to 500 MHz
  Decoupling design: bulk (100 uF), mid-freq (10 uF, 1 uF), high-freq (100 nF, 10 nF)
output:
This PDN impedance sweep reveals a significant anti-resonance peak that violates the target impedance in the critical mid-frequency range.

Detection: The impedance is well-controlled below 100 MHz, staying under 0.31 ohm — comfortably below the 0.5 ohm target. However, between 120 MHz and 200 MHz, the impedance rises sharply, peaking at 2.24 ohm at 150 MHz — more than 4x the 0.5 ohm target impedance. This is not a gradual rise due to insufficient decoupling; it is a resonant peak with the characteristic shape of an anti-resonance: sharp rise from 0.31 ohm at 100 MHz to 2.24 ohm at 150 MHz, then sharp recovery to 0.38 ohm at 200 MHz. Above 200 MHz, the impedance is marginally controlled, briefly touching 0.49 ohm at 500 MHz.

Isolation: An anti-resonance peak in a PDN impedance profile occurs at the frequency where the inductive impedance of one capacitor group equals the capacitive impedance of an adjacent group, forming a parallel LC tank circuit. At this frequency, the two groups effectively cancel each other's current delivery, creating a high-impedance node. Specifically, the 150 MHz peak sits in the gap between the mid-frequency capacitors (1 uF and 10 uF, which become inductive above 50-100 MHz due to their ESL) and the high-frequency capacitors (100 nF and 10 nF, which are still capacitive at 150 MHz but have insufficient total capacitance to maintain low impedance). The anti-resonance impedance is approximately Z_peak = sqrt(L_mid / C_high), where L_mid is the effective inductance of the mid-frequency caps at 150 MHz and C_high is the total high-frequency capacitance.

The 2.24 ohm peak means that any processor switching current with spectral content at 150 MHz will see this high impedance and produce supply voltage ripple of delta_V = I_transient * Z_peak. For a processor drawing 500 mA transient current at 150 MHz, the ripple would be 1.12V — catastrophic for a 1.0-1.2V core supply.

Recommended action: Add capacitors specifically targeting the 120-200 MHz range. The most effective solution is to add 100-470 nF capacitors with low ESL placement — X2Y or reverse-geometry (0306) packages that have ESL below 200 pH. Place them within 2 mm of the U1 VDD balls to minimize mounting inductance. Expect to need 8-12 additional capacitors to fill the impedance gap. After modification, re-measure and verify the entire 1 MHz to 1 GHz range stays below 0.5 ohm. Also verify that the fix does not create a new anti-resonance at a different frequency — the new capacitors must overlap in effective frequency range with both the existing mid-frequency and high-frequency groups.

instruction:
We ran our standard voltage margining sweep on the new board revision. Here are the functional test results at each voltage step. Nominal VCC is 3.3V. Your review is appreciated.

Voltage margining test (nominal 3.3V, +/-10% range):
  Voltage (V)    Result
        2.97     FAIL
        3.00     FAIL
        3.03     FAIL
        3.07     FAIL
        3.10     FAIL
        3.14     PASS
        3.17     PASS
        3.20     PASS
        3.23     PASS
        3.27     PASS
        3.30     PASS
        3.33     PASS
        3.37     PASS
        3.40     PASS
        3.43     PASS
        3.47     PASS
        3.50     PASS
        3.53     PASS
        3.57     PASS
        3.60     PASS
        3.63     PASS

  Spec: must pass across +/-10% range (2.97V to 3.63V)
output:
This voltage margining sweep shows a significant low-side margin deficiency that makes this board revision non-compliant with its operating specification.

Detection: The board fails at all voltage steps below 3.14V but passes at 3.14V and above. The specification requires pass across the full +/-10% range, which is 2.97V to 3.63V. The actual low-side margin is only (3.30 - 3.14) / 3.30 = 4.8%, less than half the required 10%. The high-side margin is fully intact — the board passes all the way to 3.63V with no issues. This asymmetric failure pattern (low-side only, high-side fine) is diagnostic and rules out several potential causes.

Isolation: The sharp pass/fail boundary at 3.14V with complete failure below that voltage points to a timing margin issue in the digital logic. CMOS gate delay is proportional to V_DD / (V_DD - V_th)^2, where V_th is the transistor threshold voltage. As supply voltage decreases, gate delay increases non-linearly. At some critical voltage, the accumulated delay along the longest combinational path exceeds the clock period, causing a setup time violation. The fact that the transition is sharp (3.10V fails, 3.14V passes, no intermittent zone) indicates the timing margin is consumed abruptly rather than gradually — consistent with a single critical path dominating.

Since this is a new board revision, the likely root cause is one of: (1) a layout change that increased trace length or added vias on the critical timing path, increasing propagation delay; (2) a clock distribution change that altered skew; or (3) a component change (different speed grade or V_th specification) that shifted the gate delay characteristics. The previous board revision presumably passed the full +/-10% range, so comparing the critical path timing between revisions will identify the regression.

Recommended action: Run static timing analysis (STA) on the FPGA or ASIC design at 3.0V supply with worst-case process corners to identify which path fails timing. If the design uses an FPGA, recompile with timing constraints tightened by 15% to recover margin. Review the board revision changes — any routing modifications near the clock or critical data paths are prime suspects. For production release, this board revision cannot ship with a 3.3V +/-10% specification; either fix the timing path to recover full margin or derate the specification to +/-5% low-side (minimum 3.14V), which must be reflected in the product datasheet and incoming inspection criteria.

instruction:
Production test data from the week. I pulled the Cpk trend for our critical VCC_CORE measurement. Can you verify everything looks okay?

Weekly Cpk trend — VCC_CORE measurement:
  Week    Cpk     Sigma (mV)    Mean (mV)    USL (mV)    LSL (mV)
     1    1.82       3.1         1203          1240        1160
     2    1.79       3.0         1204          1240        1160
     3    1.75       3.2         1206          1240        1160
     4    1.71       3.1         1208          1240        1160
     5    1.65       3.0         1210          1240        1160
     6    1.58       3.1         1213          1240        1160
     7    1.52       3.2         1215          1240        1160
     8    1.44       3.0         1218          1240        1160
     9    1.38       3.1         1220          1240        1160
    10    1.31       3.2         1222          1240        1160
    11    1.24       3.1         1225          1240        1160
    12    1.18       3.0         1227          1240        1160

  Threshold: Cpk >= 1.33 (4-sigma capability)
output:
This Cpk trend contains a systematic degradation that has already crossed the control threshold.

Detection: The Cpk has declined steadily from 1.82 in week 1 to 1.18 in week 12 — a drop of 0.64 units over 12 weeks. The trend crossed below the 1.33 threshold (4-sigma capability) at approximately week 10, and is continuing to decline. At the current rate of approximately 0.053 Cpk units per week, Cpk will reach 1.0 (3-sigma, or 2,700 ppm defect rate) by approximately week 15, and fall below 0.67 (2-sigma) by week 21. This is not random variation — the decline is monotonic with no reversals, indicating a deterministic assignable cause.

Isolation: The critical diagnostic clue is in the sigma and mean columns. Sigma is rock-stable at 3.0-3.2 mV across all 12 weeks — the process spread has not changed. However, the mean is drifting upward: from 1203 mV in week 1 to 1227 mV in week 12, a 24 mV increase at approximately 2 mV per week. Since Cpk = min((USL - mean), (mean - LSL)) / (3 * sigma), and the USL is fixed at 1240 mV, the Cpk decline is driven entirely by the shrinking gap between the mean and USL. At week 12, the margin to USL is only 1240 - 1227 = 13 mV = 4.2 sigma, while the margin to LSL is 1227 - 1160 = 67 mV = 21.6 sigma. The process is drifting toward the upper specification limit.

A constant drift rate with stable sigma rules out random variation and points to a deterministic, progressive mechanism. The most common causes are: (1) voltage regulator reference drift due to aging or temperature compensation shift — the output voltage is climbing slowly; (2) test fixture calibration drift — the measurement system is reading progressively higher; or (3) component parameter drift in the feedback resistor network of the voltage regulator — resistor values shifting with thermal aging or humidity exposure.

Recommended action: Immediately verify whether the drift is in the DUT or the test system. Measure VCC_CORE on 5 boards from the current lot using an independent calibrated multimeter. If the multimeter confirms 1225+ mV, the drift is real and the voltage regulator or its feedback network needs investigation. If the multimeter reads 1203-1205 mV, the drift is a test fixture calibration issue — recalibrate the test system. Either way, do not wait for the Cpk to degrade further. The process has already dropped below the 1.33 threshold, meaning defect rates are increasing and customer quality is at risk. Implement a corrective action and adjust the process center back to 1200-1205 mV.

instruction:
Ran the standard HALT step-stress on batch 7. Here are the temperature step results. 22 units per usual. Review requested.

HALT temperature step-stress — Batch 7 (22 units):
  Step (C)    Failures    Cumulative    Notes
       25          0            0
       35          0            0
       45          0            0
       55          0            0
       65          0            0
       75          1            1        Unit 14 — display blanks
       85          3            4        Units 3, 9, 17 — display blanks
       95          0            4
      105          1            5        Unit 22 — CPU hang
      115          2            7        Units 6, 11 — no boot
      125          3           10        Units 1, 8, 20 — no boot

  Operational limit: +105 C
  Destruct limit target: +115 C
  Previous batches (1-6): zero failures below +95 C
output:
This HALT data reveals an unexpected early-failure cluster that represents a significant departure from historical batch performance.

Detection: Four units failed at or below +85 C — one at +75 C and three at +85 C — all with the same failure mode (display blanks). The operational limit is +105 C, so failures at +75 C are 30 C below the expected safe operating boundary. Previous batches 1 through 6 had zero failures below +95 C, making this a clear regression. The failures above +105 C (units at 105, 115, 125 C) are within expected behavior for destruct-level stress and are not concerning. The critical issue is the 4 premature failures.

Isolation: The Arrhenius acceleration model relates failure rate to temperature through the activation energy Ea: AF = exp((Ea/k) * (1/T_use - 1/T_stress)). Previous batches failing only above +95 C implied an activation energy consistent with the design assumption — typically Ea = 0.7 eV for semiconductor junction failures. However, batch 7 failing at +75 C requires a much lower activation energy to produce failures at that stress level. Working backward, if the expected failure temperature was +105 C and we are seeing failures at +75 C, the effective activation energy for this failure mechanism is approximately 0.4 eV — consistent with surface-related degradation mechanisms such as ionic contamination, intermetallic growth, or moisture-driven corrosion rather than bulk semiconductor wear-out.

The common failure mode (display blanks) across all four early failures suggests a shared component or interface. The display subsystem likely has a specific component — a display driver IC, flex connector, or backlight driver — that is the weak link. The fact that 4 of 22 units (18%) fail early, rather than all units, indicates the failure mechanism is present in a subset of the population, possibly correlated with a specific component lot or assembly variation.

Recommended action: Perform failure analysis on units 3, 9, 14, and 17. Disassemble and inspect the display interface — look for solder joint quality on the display connector, flex cable condition, and display driver IC markings (lot/date code). Check whether these four units share a common component lot that differs from the non-failing units. Re-run HALT on 5 units from batch 6 as a control to confirm the baseline has not changed. If the early failures trace to a component lot, quarantine remaining batch 7 inventory and work with the supplier to characterize the lot difference. The 0.4 eV activation energy suggests the failure mechanism will eventually manifest in field operation at much lower temperatures — this is a reliability risk that cannot be dispositioned without root cause.

instruction:
Here's the weekly yield summary from the line. Normal production, same board, same test program. Just looking for your standard read of the data.

Weekly first-pass yield (FPY) with lot traceability:
  Week    FPY (%)    Lot Code      Volume    Top Fail Mode
     1     98.7      2026-W10       412      —
     2     98.4      2026-W11       395      —
     3     99.1      2026-W12       428      —
     4     98.8      2026-W13       401      —
     5     83.2      2026-W14       389      U5 Icc high (62 of 65 fails)
     6     85.6      2026-W15       410      U5 Icc high (54 of 59 fails)
     7     97.8      2026-W16       422      —
     8     98.5      2026-W17       387      —
     9     98.9      2026-W18       415      —
    10     98.3      2026-W19       403      —

  Target: FPY > 98% sustained
output:
This yield trend data contains a clear excursion that has already resolved, but requires root cause closure to prevent recurrence.

Detection: Weeks 1-4 and 7-10 maintain FPY at 97.8-99.1%, well above the 98% target. However, weeks 5 and 6 show a dramatic drop to 83.2% and 85.6% respectively — a 15-point yield loss affecting approximately 120 boards across roughly 800 units tested. The yield drop is not gradual; it appears suddenly in week 5 and recovers just as suddenly in week 7. The top fail mode in weeks 5-6 is overwhelmingly "U5 Icc high," accounting for 62 of 65 fails in week 5 (95%) and 54 of 59 fails in week 6 (92%). In all other weeks, no single failure mode dominates, consistent with normal random defect background.

Isolation: The combination of sudden onset, sudden recovery, single-component failure mode, and lot-code correlation points directly to a component lot issue with U5. Weeks 5-6 correspond to lot codes 2026-W14 and 2026-W15, while the recovery in week 7 (2026-W16) coincides with a lot change. The physics is straightforward: U5's quiescent current (Icc) is determined by its internal bias circuitry, which is set during semiconductor fabrication. A process shift at the U5 supplier — such as a threshold voltage drift, doping variation, or gate oxide thickness change — can shift the entire lot's Icc distribution upward. If the shifted mean pushes a significant fraction of the population above the test limit, yield drops precipitously. The fact that sigma is unchanged (the failure rate is 15-17%, not 50% or 100%) indicates the lot mean shifted enough to push the upper tail of the distribution past the Icc test limit, but the bulk of the population still passes.

The abrupt recovery in week 7 confirms this is component-lot-correlated, not a process drift or equipment issue, which would persist until corrected.

Recommended action: Contact the U5 supplier with the specific lot codes (2026-W14 and 2026-W15) and request their outgoing test data for Icc on those lots. Determine whether their process control detected the shift and whether corrective action was taken. Pull the failed boards from weeks 5-6 inventory and characterize U5 Icc on a sample — determine if the parts are out of the device specification or if the board-level test limit is tighter than the device spec. If the parts are within device spec but failing board-level limits, the design guard-band for U5 Icc is insufficient and needs widening. Implement incoming inspection sampling for U5 Icc to catch future lot shifts before they reach production.

instruction:
Standard Weibull analysis from our latest HALT batch. Two batches compared as usual — baseline from qualification and current production. 20 units each. Here's the summary.

Weibull analysis — HALT comparison:
  Batch A (qualification baseline, 20 units):
    Beta (shape):  3.45
    Eta (characteristic life): 5,200 hours
    B10 life: 2,340 hours
    R-squared: 0.978

  Batch B (current production, 20 units):
    Beta (shape):  1.72
    Eta (characteristic life): 6,800 hours
    B10 life: 2,580 hours
    R-squared: 0.961

  Both batches tested under identical HALT protocol: 85 C / 85% RH, 1.5x rated voltage
output:
This Weibull comparison shows a fundamental change in the failure mechanism that is masked by the similar B10 life values.

Detection: At first glance, batch B appears comparable or even slightly better than batch A — the B10 life improved from 2,340 to 2,580 hours, and the characteristic life eta increased from 5,200 to 6,800 hours. However, the shape parameter beta dropped dramatically from 3.45 to 1.72. This nearly 50% reduction in beta is the critical anomaly. The Weibull beta parameter encodes the physics of the failure mechanism: beta > 2 indicates wear-out failures with an increasing hazard rate (bathtub curve right side), beta = 1 indicates random failures with a constant hazard rate (bathtub curve bottom), and beta < 1 indicates infant mortality with a decreasing hazard rate. Batch A's beta of 3.45 represents a well-behaved wear-out mode — parts survive for a predictable duration then fail as a physical degradation mechanism accumulates. Batch B's beta of 1.72 is drifting toward random failure behavior.

Isolation: A beta shift from 3.45 to 1.72 while eta increases indicates the failure population has shifted from a single dominant wear-out mechanism to a mixture of competing mechanisms. When multiple failure modes with different activation energies compete, the Weibull plot becomes a composite of their individual distributions, which reduces the apparent beta toward 1 (even if each individual mode has beta > 3). The higher eta compensates statistically — the median life is longer because no single mechanism dominates — but the reduced beta means failures are less predictable. Some units will fail much earlier than expected based on the batch A wear-out model, while others will last much longer.

Physically, the 85 C/85% RH stress activates moisture-related degradation mechanisms (corrosion, ionic migration, delamination) alongside thermal wear-out (electromigration, intermetallic growth). If a material or process change in batch B introduced a new moisture-sensitive failure path — for example, a different mold compound, die attach adhesive, or passivation quality — the new mechanism would compete with the original wear-out mode, producing the observed mixed-mode beta.

Recommended action: This cannot be dispositioned on B10 life alone. Perform failure analysis on at least 5 failed units from batch B to categorize failure modes. If two or more distinct mechanisms are found (e.g., corrosion plus electromigration), each needs its own Weibull fit to determine individual beta and eta values. Compare the bill of materials and supplier lot codes between batches A and B — any change in encapsulant, die attach, leadframe plating, or substrate material is a prime suspect. The lower R-squared for batch B (0.961 vs 0.978) also supports the mixed-mode hypothesis, as a single Weibull fit poorly represents a multi-mechanism population.

instruction:
Eye diagram measurements from the latest SerDes qualification on the 25 Gbps backplane link. Both pre- and post-equalization captures. Routine check.

Eye diagram — 25 Gbps NRZ, Channel 5, 100,000 UI acquisition:

  Pre-equalization:
    Eye height:      38 mV
    Eye width:       11.2 ps
    Random jitter:   1.8 ps RMS
    Determ. jitter:  22.4 ps
    Total jitter:    47.6 ps (at BER 1e-12)

  Post-equalization (CTLE + 3-tap DFE):
    Eye height:      61 mV
    Eye width:       18.5 ps
    Random jitter:   1.9 ps RMS
    Determ. jitter:  14.1 ps
    Total jitter:    40.7 ps (at BER 1e-12)

  Spec limits (post-EQ): eye height > 50 mV, TJ < 45 ps
  UI at 25 Gbps: 40 ps
output:
This eye diagram data passes the post-equalization specification numerically, but the margins are thin and the pre-equalization eye reveals an underlying channel problem.

Detection: The post-equalization eye height of 61 mV passes the 50 mV spec with only 11 mV of margin (22% above minimum). The total jitter of 40.7 ps passes the 45 ps limit with only 4.3 ps margin (the UI is 40 ps, so the timing margin is effectively 40 - 40.7 = -0.7 ps if we consider the full UI window — this eye is barely open). More concerning is the pre-equalization eye: 38 mV height and 11.2 ps width at 25 Gbps represents a severely closed eye. The equalizer is doing heavy lifting — CTLE + 3-tap DFE recovers only 23 mV of eye height and 7.3 ps of width, which is near the practical limit of what equalization can achieve. The deterministic jitter dominates at 22.4 ps pre-EQ, and the equalizer only reduces it to 14.1 ps — a 37% reduction. Typically, a well-designed 25 Gbps channel with proper impedance control should show DJ below 15 ps pre-equalization.

Isolation: The high deterministic jitter of 22.4 ps pre-EQ is the root problem. DJ at this level indicates significant inter-symbol interference (ISI) from impedance discontinuities and frequency-dependent loss in the channel. At 25 Gbps, the Nyquist frequency is 12.5 GHz, and the channel must maintain controlled impedance and low loss up to at least the third harmonic (37.5 GHz would be ideal, but 20 GHz is the practical minimum for NRZ). The fact that the equalizer reduces DJ by only 37% suggests the channel has both distributed loss (which CTLE can partially compensate) and localized reflections (which are harder to equalize). Via stubs, connector transitions, and AC coupling capacitor parasitics are the usual sources of localized DJ in backplane channels.

The random jitter is well-controlled at 1.8-1.9 ps RMS, confirming that the power supply and reference clock are clean — this is purely a channel quality issue.

Recommended action: This channel passes specification but has insufficient margin for production. Temperature and aging will degrade the eye further — expect 5-10 mV eye height loss at worst-case temperature. Measure S21 insertion loss and S11 return loss from 100 MHz to 20 GHz to identify the specific frequency-dependent loss and reflection points. Check for via stub resonances (look for notches in S21) and connector return loss peaks. If the backplane design allows, back-drill connector vias to reduce stub length. Consider moving to PAM4 modulation at 12.5 Gbaud if NRZ margin cannot be recovered, though this requires different SerDes configuration.

instruction:
Just finished the ESR measurement run on today's production lot. 300 boards tested. C47 is the 100 nF decoupling cap next to the main processor. Here's the distribution summary.

ESR measurements — C47 (100 nF MLCC, X7R, 0402) across 300 boards:
  Distribution: bimodal
    Population A: mean = 0.72 ohm, sigma = 0.11 ohm (58% of units, 174 boards)
    Population B: mean = 1.38 ohm, sigma = 0.13 ohm (42% of units, 126 boards)
  Combined mean: 1.00 ohm
  Spec limit: ESR < 2.5 ohm
  No individual readings exceed spec limit
  Previous lots: unimodal, mean = 0.75 ohm, sigma = 0.12 ohm
output:
This ESR distribution has a clear bimodal signature that indicates a component mixing issue despite all individual readings passing specification.

Detection: The historical baseline for C47 is a unimodal Gaussian distribution centered at 0.75 ohm with sigma of 0.12 ohm. The current lot shows two distinct populations: Population A at 0.72 ohm (consistent with historical data) and Population B at 1.38 ohm (92% higher than Population A). The separation between peaks is (1.38 - 0.72) / sqrt(0.11^2 + 0.13^2) = 3.9 sigma — meaning there is essentially zero overlap between the populations, confirming two distinct component sources. While all readings are within the 2.5 ohm spec limit, the presence of an unexpected second population is a quality control red flag.

Isolation: ESR in an MLCC is determined by the dielectric loss tangent: ESR = tan(delta) / (2 * pi * f * C), where tan(delta) is a material property of the ceramic dielectric. The X7R dielectric formulation has a specific barium titanate composition that determines tan(delta). Different ceramic suppliers — or different formulations from the same supplier — will have different tan(delta) values, producing different ESR measurements on the same capacitance value. A 92% difference in ESR (0.72 vs 1.38 ohm) implies a nearly 2x difference in dielectric loss tangent, which is too large to be a normal lot-to-lot variation from a single source. This is consistent with two different component suppliers or two different dielectric formulations being mixed on the same pick-and-place reel.

The 58/42 population split suggests a reel splice point where a secondary reel was spliced onto the primary reel during the surface mount assembly process. If the secondary reel came from a different supplier or lot, the mixed population results.

Recommended action: Trace the component lot codes on the reels used for C47 during this production run. Verify whether a reel splice occurred and whether the spliced reel came from a different supplier or lot. While the higher-ESR population (1.38 ohm) is within specification, it provides less effective high-frequency decoupling — the impedance minimum of the capacitor (which occurs at its series resonant frequency) is higher, meaning the decoupling effectiveness at high frequencies is reduced by nearly 2x for those boards. Evaluate whether the 1.38 ohm ESR affects the PDN impedance target at the processor. If it does, segregate the two populations and potentially rework the Population B boards. Implement incoming inspection for ESR on this component to catch future mixing events before boards are assembled.

instruction:
Captured the PLL lock acquisition waveform during debug. Control voltage and VCO output frequency over 500 microseconds. The PLL is supposed to lock to 2.4 GHz from a 100 MHz reference. Here's the data.

PLL lock acquisition — target: 2.4 GHz, reference: 100 MHz:
  Time (us)    Vctrl (V)    VCO freq (GHz)
         0      1.65          2.38
        25      0.82          2.12
        50      2.48          2.68
        75      0.91          2.15
       100      2.41          2.65
       125      0.85          2.13
       150      2.45          2.67
       175      0.88          2.14
       200      2.43          2.66
       225      0.84          2.13
       250      2.46          2.67
       275      0.87          2.14
       300      2.44          2.66
       325      0.85          2.13
       350      2.45          2.67
       375      0.86          2.14
       400      2.44          2.66
       425      0.85          2.13
       450      2.45          2.67
       475      0.86          2.14
       500      2.44          2.66

Expected behavior: control voltage settles to ~1.65V within 100 us, VCO locks to 2.400 GHz.
output:
This PLL waveform shows a textbook loop instability — the PLL never acquires lock and oscillates indefinitely.

Detection: The expected behavior is for the control voltage to settle from its initial value to approximately 1.65V within 100 microseconds, with the VCO locking to exactly 2.400 GHz. Instead, the control voltage oscillates between approximately 0.84V and 2.46V with a period of about 50 microseconds (20 kHz oscillation frequency). The VCO frequency correspondingly swings between 2.13 and 2.67 GHz — a 540 MHz span centered near the target but never converging. The oscillation amplitude is not decaying over the 500 us capture; the peaks at t=450 and t=500 are the same magnitude as at t=50 and t=100. This sustained, constant-amplitude oscillation is the hallmark of a control loop with zero or negative phase margin.

Isolation: PLL stability requires sufficient phase margin — typically greater than 45 degrees — at the loop bandwidth frequency. The loop filter, consisting of resistors and capacitors between the charge pump output and the VCO control input, sets the poles and zeros that determine phase margin. The oscillation period of 50 us (20 kHz) indicates the loop bandwidth is near this frequency, which for a 100 MHz reference PLL with a divide ratio of 24 is plausible.

The most common cause of PLL instability with this exact symptom — sustained oscillation with no lock — is an incorrect capacitor value in the loop filter. The main integrating capacitor C1 in the loop filter determines the dominant pole. If C1 is too small (for example, 100 pF installed instead of the designed 10 nF), the pole frequency moves 100x higher than intended. This eliminates the phase lag that C1 is supposed to provide at low frequencies, pushes the unity-gain crossover frequency far above the design point, and reduces phase margin to zero or below. The result is the sustained oscillation we observe.

The VCO gain K_VCO can be estimated from the data: frequency swing of 540 MHz over a voltage swing of 1.62V gives K_VCO = 333 MHz/V, which is typical for a 2.4 GHz VCO.

Recommended action: Measure the loop filter component values in-circuit. Check C1 (the integrating capacitor) — verify it matches the design value, which should be in the 1-10 nF range for this loop bandwidth. A 100 pF capacitor in a 0402 package can be easily confused with a 10 nF during assembly if the marking is ambiguous. If C1 is correct, measure C2 (the ripple-smoothing capacitor) and R1 (the zero-setting resistor). If this is a new board revision, compare the BOM against the loop filter design spreadsheet and verify that the component designators were not swapped. Once the correct capacitor is installed, the PLL should lock within 100 us with well-damped settling.

instruction:
Board-level functional test results from a 1,000-unit production run. Per-step pass/fail with timestamps. Could you review the data for any trends?

Functional Test Results — 1,000 consecutive boards:
  Step    Description              Pass    Fail    Fail Rate
    1     Power sequencing          1000      0      0.000%
    2     Clock verification        1000      0      0.000%
    3     CPU boot                  1000      0      0.000%
    4     Memory init                999      1      0.100%
    5     Memory pattern test        998      2      0.200%
    6     PCIe link training        1000      0      0.000%
    7     USB enumeration           1000      0      0.000%
    8     Ethernet loopback         1000      0      0.000%
    9     GPIO toggling             1000      0      0.000%
   10     SPI flash read/verify     1000      0      0.000%
   11     I2C sensor poll           1000      0      0.000%
   12     ADC channel sweep          978     22      2.200%
   13     DAC output verify         1000      0      0.000%
   14     Watchdog timer test       1000      0      0.000%
   15     Final power-down           1000      0      0.000%

Step 12 fail log: all 22 failures on ADC channel 3 only (channels 0-2, 4-7 pass)
Timestamp analysis: failures distributed randomly across the run (not clustered)
output:
This production test data shows a single-channel failure pattern that isolates the problem to a specific signal path.

Detection: Of 15 test steps, only step 12 (ADC channel sweep) shows a meaningful failure rate at 2.2% (22 of 1,000 boards). Steps 4 and 5 have 1-2 failures which are within normal background noise for memory tests (0.1-0.2% is typical for marginal DRAM cells). The 2.2% failure rate on step 12 is an order of magnitude above background and is statistically significant. The critical detail from the fail log is that all 22 failures occur exclusively on ADC channel 3 — the other seven channels (0-2, 4-7) pass on every single board. This single-channel selectivity eliminates the ADC silicon itself as the root cause, because an ADC defect would typically affect all channels or show up as a linearity/noise issue across the converter.

Isolation: Since channel 3 fails while channels 0-7 share the same ADC die, the problem must be in the signal path unique to channel 3 — the PCB trace from the analog source to the ADC channel 3 input pin, the input protection components (ESD diodes, RC filter), and any analog mux or buffer in that path. A 2.2% failure rate with random time distribution (not clustered) indicates a marginal condition rather than a hard defect. If it were a hard open or short, the failure rate would be much higher (approaching 100% for that channel).

The physics points to signal integrity on the channel 3 input trace. ADC inputs are high-impedance nodes (typically > 1 Mohm), making them extremely sensitive to crosstalk and noise coupling. If the channel 3 trace runs adjacent to a digital signal (clock, data bus, or GPIO), capacitive crosstalk can inject noise that causes the ADC reading to exceed the test tolerance window on some fraction of boards. Board-to-board variation in trace impedance, solder joint quality on the input filter components, and slight component value variations would modulate the coupling margin, explaining why 2.2% fail while 97.8% pass — the boards that fail are at the tail of the noise margin distribution.

Recommended action: Review the channel 3 PCB layout — check for digital signal traces running parallel to the channel 3 analog input within 10 mils. Compare the routing of channel 3 against channels 0-2 (which pass) to identify any layout difference. Measure the actual ADC channel 3 noise floor on a failing board using an oscilloscope at the ADC input pin — look for coupled noise spikes. If crosstalk is confirmed, add a series RC filter (100 ohm + 100 pF) at the channel 3 input if not already present, or increase trace spacing. For immediate production, widen the test tolerance for channel 3 only if analysis confirms the noise does not affect functional accuracy in the application, or add a retest step that averages multiple ADC readings to filter out the intermittent noise.

      14       50.51

      16       50.48
